figures.gloss: The supreme creator and ultimate source of all existence. He is absolute, transcendent, and formless, generating the vital energy of the universe but demanding no direct offerings, altars, or ritual sacrifices.
figures.facet: Because his pure power is too vast and remote for human interaction, all terrestrial affairs are strictly delegated to the Orixas, who act as his active, earthly emissaries.
figures.provenance: Yoruba cosmology preserved largely intact in the Ketu nation; Pierre Verger's 'Orixas'.

figures.gloss: The dynamic guardian of the crossroads and the essential messenger between humans and the Orixas. He is the master of movement, chaos, communication, and the undeniable kinetic friction of life.
figures.facet: He must receive the very first offering in every ritual; without his compliance, no axe can flow. Despite historical demonization by the Church, he is fundamentally amoral, not evil.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Orisha Esu; heavily rehabilitated from colonial Catholic demonology in modern Candomble praxis.

figures.gloss: The eldest and most venerated Orixa, tasked by Olorun with molding the physical bodies of humanity. Draped in pure white, he embodies profound wisdom, patience, and absolute peace.
figures.facet: His pristine purity means he strictly abhors blood, palm oil (dende), and violence. His intense syncretism with Jesus ('Senhor do Bonfim') forms the central, unshakeable pillar of Bahian identity.
figures.provenance: Obatala of the Yoruba; deeply syncretized with 'Nosso Senhor do Bonfim' in Bahia.

figures.gloss: The maternal sovereign of the ocean and the ultimate mother to many of the Orixas. She represents fertility, the protection of families, and the vast, nurturing, and terrifying deep.
figures.facet: In Brazil, she transformed from a regional river deity into the absolute sovereign of the Atlantic Ocean, profoundly symbolizing the traumatic, watery passage of enslaved Africans.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Yemoja (Ogun River); syncretized universally in Brazil with the Virgin Mary ('Nossa Senhora dos Navegantes').

figures.gloss: The fierce Orixa of iron, warfare, and technology. He is the relentless path-opener who forged the first tools for agriculture, hunting, and human survival.
figures.facet: While Exu opens the metaphysical crossroads, Ogum physically clears the literal roads. He is the kinetic force of human civilization and survival under harsh diasporic conditions.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ogun; syncretized with Saint Anthony in Bahia and Saint George in Rio de Janeiro.

figures.gloss: The proud, royal Orixa of thunder, lightning, and absolute justice. He wields the oxe (double-headed axe) and rules over the structural laws and equilibrium of human society.
figures.facet: He does not mete out blind vengeance but calculated, structural justice. He embodies the lost royalty of African kingdoms, restoring dignity and divine fairness to his initiates.
figures.provenance: Deified fourth king of the Oyo Empire (Sango); most commonly syncretized with Saint Jerome in Bahian Ketu houses, though regional practice also links him to São João Batista (notably in Xangô do Recife); his wives Iansã and Oxum carry their own separate syncretisms (Santa Bárbara, Nossa Senhora da Conceição).

figures.gloss: The fundamental life force, dynamic energy, and sacred power that animates the universe. It is the blood of the cosmos, residing physically in leaves, stones, animals, and the spoken word.
figures.facet: Axe is a thermodynamic spiritual currency. It is not static; it must be actively cultivated, fed, and circulated through ritual sacrifice and rhythmic drumming to prevent depletion.
figures.provenance: Central Yoruba philosophical concept ('ase'), foundational across all Candomble nations.

figures.gloss: The sacred temple complex where Candomble is practiced. It is a consecrated territory divided into public dance floors (barracao) and secret shrines (pejis) housing the physical manifestations of the Orixas.
figures.facet: The terreiro operates as a sovereign micro-cosmos, physically re-creating the lost African homelands on Brazilian soil and providing a profound spatial anchor for diaspora identity.
figures.provenance: Developed in 19th-century Bahia; documented extensively by Ruth Landes in 'The City of Women'.

figures.gloss: The physical possession of an initiate (iao) by their ruling Orixa during ritual ceremonies. Driven by specific drum rhythms, the spirit descends to dance and interact directly with the community.
figures.facet: Unlike mere spiritual inspiration, the medium's consciousness is completely suppressed. The Orixa wears specific vestments and performs ancestral choreographies, proving the gods are materially present.
figures.provenance: Core praxis of Candomble; analyzed by Roger Bastide in 'The African Religions of Brazil'.

figures.gloss: Orixá of rivers and sweet water, and of gold, beauty, love, and fertility; mistress of the abebé (mirror-fan) and patron of the merindilogun cowrie divination.
figures.facet: Beauty, wealth, and oracular knowledge are treated as facets of the same river-given power rather than separate domains — her mirror and her shells are both instruments of seeing clearly.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Òṣun of the Osun River (Osogbo); Pierre Verger's fieldwork on Bahian Orixá cults; syncretized with Nossa Senhora da Conceição in Bahia.

figures.gloss: Orixá of winds, tempests, and lightning, and mistress of the Niger river; the fierce warrior-wife of Xangô, said in some tellings to have once held power over fire and thunder herself.
figures.facet: She is the sole Orixá granted authority over the eguns (spirits of the dead), commanding them with her eruexim fly-whisk — making her the tradition's necessary intermediary whenever the ancestral dead must be approached.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ọya of the Niger River; syncretized with Santa Bárbara in Bahia; documented in Verger's Orixás and Bastide's ethnography of the Ketu nation.

figures.gloss: The hunter Orixá of the forest, game, and abundance, armed with the ofá (bow and arrow); as legendary king of Ketu, he is the patron deity of the Ketu nation that dominates Bahian Candomblé.
figures.facet: He converts the wild forest into a provisioning ground for the community, making the hunter's discipline and the forest's axé the material basis of a terreiro's prosperity.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ọ̀ṣọ́ọ̀sì of the Kingdom of Ketu; Verger's Orixás; commonly syncretized with São Jorge or São Sebastião depending on the house.

figures.gloss: Orixá of smallpox, epidemic disease, and their cure, whose body is covered in sores and whose face is hidden beneath a raffia mask (filá); he is petitioned as fiercely to withhold sickness as to send it.
figures.facet: His hidden, ravaged body makes him the one Orixá whose power is inseparable from bodily affliction itself — he does not rule disease from outside it, he wears it.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ṣọ̀npọ̀nná/Obalúayé; the Jeje-Nagô Omolu/Obaluaiê complex documented by Verger and Bastide; honored on Mondays with popcorn (pipoca/doburu).

figures.gloss: The eldest of the water Orixás, goddess of primordial mud, still swamp waters, and death; she predates the age of iron, and her rites forbid metal blades — her emblem is the ibiri, a beaded staff of woven straw.
figures.facet: As the only Orixá whose cult remains explicitly bladeless, she preserves in ritual form a stratum of religion older than the iron-working cults of Ogum and the hunt.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Nàná Bùkùú, cognate with the Fon Nana Buluku (Herskovits, Verger); syncretized with Sant'Ana (Saint Anne) in Bahia.

figures.gloss: Orixá of the rainbow and of cyclical continuity, who carries water from the earth to the sky in serpent form; he spends six months of the year male and six female, uniting the wet and dry seasons in one body.
figures.facet: His seasonal sex-change makes gendered complementarity, not a fixed sex, the very mechanism by which the rains and the dry season alternate.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ọ̀ṣùmàrè, cognate with the Fon serpent-deity Dan/Aido-Hwedo (Herskovits, Dahomean Narrative); syncretized with São Bartolomeu in Bahia.

figures.gloss: The one-legged, one-armed, one-eyed Orixá of sacred leaves and herbal medicine, sole keeper of the botanical knowledge that gives every other Orixá's rites their axé.
figures.facet: The liturgical maxim 'without leaves there is no Orixá' makes him structurally prior to the entire pantheon in practice, however minor his mythic profile — no possession, cleansing, or offering functions without his leaves first being cut and consecrated.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ọ̀sanyìn; Pierre Verger's botanical fieldwork on Candomblé leaf-lore (Ewé: os usos das plantas na sociedade iorubá).

figures.gloss: The sacred child-twins, honored with sweets and small toys as protectors of children and of twins born into the community; they are venerated together and never divided in ritual.
figures.facet: Their cult localizes the historically high Yoruba twin-birth rate into a theology in which duplication itself is sacred rather than anomalous.
figures.provenance: Yoruba Ìbejì twin-veneration, transplanted into Bahian Candomblé; documented in Verger's Orixás.

figures.gloss: The initiated priestess or priest who heads a terreiro, custodian of its axé and the authority who conducts each initiate's (iaô's) passage into full standing.
figures.facet: Public figures such as Mãe Menininha do Gantois turned this office into Candomblé's civic face, giving an initiatory religion built on secrecy a recognized public authority in 20th-century Bahia.
figures.provenance: Terreiro governance documented by Ruth Landes (The City of Women) and Roger Bastide; Mãe Menininha do Gantois's public role in Bahia.

figures.gloss: The masked ancestral dead, honored in the Baba Egum cult kept deliberately separate from Orixá worship; on Itaparica island especially, they are handled only by specialist ojés, never by Orixá mediums.
figures.facet: The strict separation of Egum veneration from Orixá incorporação draws a doctrinal line the roster otherwise elides: ancestor-cult and deity-cult are related but administratively and ritually distinct systems.
figures.provenance: Baba Egum cult of Itaparica island; documented by Bastide in The African Religions of Brazil.

figures.gloss: Divination by sixteen cowrie shells, cast and read by the priesthood to identify a person's ruling Orixá, diagnose misfortune, and discern the will of the gods in everyday consultation.
figures.facet: It is Candomblé's ordinary oracular channel, consulted far more often than possession itself — making búzios, not trance, the daily working interface between devotee and Orixá.
figures.provenance: Yoruba merindilogun/dilogun cowrie divination; documented by Pierre Verger and in Bastide's ethnography of Candomblé praxis.

edges.note: Exu holds the metaphysical crossroads, while Ogum physically clears the path; they are a bound dyad always honored sequentially.
The terreiro serves as the vital physical battery and containment field where axe is ritually generated, stored, and distributed.
Often paired structurally as the great aquatic mother and the supreme white father, generating the foundation of the Bahian pantheon.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba trickster and messenger, retaining his absolute role as the opener of paths, adapted directly into the Brazilian diaspora.
GOLD SEAM: Parallel entities of the crossroads forged into structurally identical roles across New World diaspora traditions.
GOLD SEAM: Both are divine messengers, masters of boundaries, and trickster guides who move freely between the realms of gods and men.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme maternal sovereigns of the ocean, revered for their vast fertility and their ultimate power over the watery deep.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba lord of iron and war transplanted directly, retaining his metallurgy, machete, and path-clearing kinetic energy.
GOLD SEAM: Shares the exact attributes of iron, warfare, and technological mastery with the Dahomean Gu.
GOLD SEAM: The Yoruba king of Oyo, perfectly preserved in Brazil as the royal wielder of lightning and absolute justice.
GOLD SEAM: Proud, robust storm-wielders who command thunder, wield iconic, culturally foundational axes or hammers, and champion structural order.
GOLD SEAM: Through intense historical syncretism, the Yoruba creator of bodies became physically identified with Jesus ('Senhor do Bonfim') in Bahia.
GOLD SEAM: Serene craftsman deities fundamentally tasked with sculpting the physical bodies and forms of living beings.
GOLD SEAM: The transcendent Yoruba creator mapped directly onto the Abrahamic God under the intense historical pressure of Brazilian Catholicism.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, transcendent African creators who originated the cosmos but withdrew from direct daily interference in human affairs.
GOLD SEAM: Both are foundational, non-anthropomorphic conceptions of a pervasive spiritual life-force that animates all phenomena and can be harnessed.
GOLD SEAM: Traditions where the ultimate, load-bearing ritual act is the physical, kinetic embodiment of a deity through human vessels.
GOLD SEAM: Community-driven rhythmic technologies designed to fundamentally alter consciousness and draw potent spiritual energy into physical space.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a thunder-lord propitiated both to protect the household and to strike down enemies on command.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: remote, senior sky-fathers who preside over the pantheon in principle but delegate active, dynamic governance to nearer, younger gods.

both traditions maintain a distinct ancestor-cult, administratively separate from worship of the high gods, in which the honored royal or ancestral dead are ritually fed and invoked to bless the living community.
plague deities of entirely unconnected traditions whose arrows or sores are both weapon and cure, invoked to inflict epidemic disease and to withhold it.
The storm-couple: Iansã, mistress of winds and the dead, is the fierce warrior-wife of Xangô, lord of thunder.
Often venerated as close brothers across the Ketu and Angola nations, sharing the iron-and-forest frontier of provisioning.
In some houses' genealogy Omolu (Obaluaiê) is reckoned the son of Nanã Buruku, tying the disease-god to the eldest water-mother; genealogy varies by nation, so kept structural rather than definitive.
Ossaim's sacred leaves are gathered within Oxóssi's forest domain; the herbalist and the hunter share the same wild terrain of axé.
The priest or priestess is the terreiro's living custodian and ritual authority; the office and the house are definitionally bound together.

Egum ancestor-veneration is administratively and ritually distinct from Orixá incorporação — handled by specialist ojés, never by Orixá mediums — marking the tradition's clean split between ancestor-cult and deity-cult.
The tradition's two channels for the Orixás' will: búzios divination for everyday guidance, incorporação for direct embodied presence.

dual-natured rainbow serpents who carry water between earth and sky and ensure cosmic continuity, from the shared Fon-Yoruba coastal West African religious complex feeding both Dahomean Vodun and Candomblé.

independently arising rainbow-serpent figures — the Afro-Brazilian and Australian Aboriginal traditions have no historical contact — sharing the widespread structural pairing of the serpent with water, cycles, and continuity.
the elder, primordial water-and-mud deity of the Fon pantheon, carried into Candomblé's Jeje-Nagô synthesis under an almost identical name.
the same earth-and-smallpox god-complex shared by neighboring Fon and Yoruba coastal religion, fused in Candomblé's Jeje-Nagô houses much as Ogum absorbs the Fon Gu.
hunter deities of the forest and its game, armed with the bow, whose favor a community's provisioning depends on.
within Brazil itself, an Afro-Brazilian and an Indigenous Amazonian master-of-the-hunt figure coexist as structurally parallel keepers of the forest's game.
West African-descended divinatory systems for discerning the gods' will, using different technical media — cowrie shells versus Fa's divining chain.

Cross-tradition seam: both are living possession-complexes in which a ritually prepared human body is directly mounted by a deity who then speaks and acts through the medium for the community — reached independently in Kerala and Afro-Brazilian religion.

West African sea-headed vodun and the Middle Passage's Iemanja both make a single sovereign of the ocean's danger and abundance — no direct attested descent from Agbe specifically, structural rhyme only.
Both ground diviner/medium authority in trance possession by a specific named spirit rather than in interpretive skill alone.
Oxalá is the Brazilian Portuguese rendering of the same orisha, showing three independent New World lines descending from one Yoruba source.
both stage the devotee's body as overtaken by sacred power in the course of worship, though Pentecostalism admits only the one Spirit where Candomblé admits many orixás.
The same Yoruba-derived Orixá of the sea, venerated under the same name and iconography in Candomblé and its Kardecist-inflected offshoot Umbanda.
The same warrior Orixá of iron worked in both traditions, with Umbanda adding the specific St. George syncretism.
The same thunder-and-justice Orixá across both traditions; Umbanda additionally overlays St. Jerome.
Olorun/Olodumare preserved in Brazilian Candomblé as the remote high god behind the orixás.
Obatala survives in Candomblé as Oxalá, the White Father, retaining the creation role and the white-cloth taboo.
Yemoja preserved in Brazil as Iemanjá, Queen of the Ocean, one of the most widely venerated orixás in Candomblé.
àṣẹ carried into Brazilian Candomblé as axé, the vital force underlying every rite of the terreiro.

The same Yoruba Yemoja carried by the transatlantic slave trade into parallel Catholic-syncretized diasporas — Brazilian Candomblé's Atlantic-sovereign Iemanjá (Navegantes) and Cuban Santería's mother of waters Yemayá (Regla).

Parallel, independent transmissions of the same withdrawn Yoruba supreme creator carried across the Middle Passage to Brazil and Cuba respectively — same origin, no direct link between the two diaspora lines themselves.
Umbanda draws its supreme creator from the Ketu-lineage Olorun preserved in Candomblé, then additionally fuses him with the Bantu Nzambi — Candomblé's own houses do not make that further merger.
Parallel diaspora transmissions of the same Yoruba Obatalá — owner of heads and white cloth, full myth-cycle including his drunken failures — carried independently to Brazil and Cuba.

Umbanda takes the Candomblé Oxalá and strips his myth-cycle (including the drunken failures), remaking him as a flawless Kardecist Christ-avatar and functionally the supreme active deity — a deliberate theological rewrite, not simple transmission.

dossier.essence: An Afro-Brazilian initiatory religion forged by enslaved West and West-Central Africans and their descendants in Bahia — rooted in the colonial slave trade and taking institutional shape in the early nineteenth century — organized in autonomous temple communities (terreiros) grouped by 'nation': Ketu (Yoruba orixás), Jeje (Fon voduns), and Angola (Bantu inquices). It centers on serving the orixás, personified powers of nature and human life, through initiation, offering, and trance. Census declarations understate it — stigma long suppressed open affiliation — but its practice and cultural reach extend across Brazil.
dossier.cosmos: Olorun (Olodumare), the remote supreme creator, delegates the working of the world to the orixás, who are sustained and moved by axé, the transferable vital force concentrated in blood, leaves, stones, and consecrated objects. Each person belongs to a particular orixá who rules their head and shapes their disposition and destiny. At death the axexê funeral rite disengages the person's axé and spirit from the community; the eguns (spirits of the dead) are honored but kept ritually segregated from the orixá cult.
dossier.practice: Entry is by divination and initiation: the iaô undergoes seclusion and consecration to their orixá, emerging into a lifelong hierarchy under a mãe- or pai-de-santo (ialorixá/babalorixá). Regular obligations include ebó (offerings, including animal sacrifice), drum-driven public festivals in which initiates incorporate their orixás in possession trance, and cowrie-shell divination (jogo de búzios). The demands are concrete: food taboos, ritual duties, and seniority counted in years since initiation.
dossier.canon: No scripture: a liturgical-oral corpus of songs, praise-names, and orixá narratives in ritual Yoruba, Fon, and Bantu-derived language, transmitted within terreiros; ethnographic compendia such as Verger's 'Orixás' (1981) serve as reference works, not scripture.
dossier.reflects: Religion as embodied memory — a transplanted pantheon kept alive under slavery by seating the gods in human bodies through initiation and trance.
sources: Roger Bastide, 'The African Religions of Brazil' (1960; Eng. tr. 1978)
Pierre Fatumbi Verger, 'Orixás: Deuses Iorubás na África e no Novo Mundo' (1981)
